God speaks to people in many ways: through their conscience, through events that happen around them, and by speaking directly. We should pay careful attention to all these things, but God never spoke as clearly as he did when he gave the Ten Commandments. He had given this law to humanity before; it was written in their hearts, but sin damaged it so much that it needed to be refreshed. The law is spiritual, and understands the secret thoughts, desires, and intentions of the heart. Its main requirement is love, without which outward obedience is just hypocrisy. It demands perfect, reliable, constant obedience; no law allows disobedience to itself. Whoever keeps all of the law but fails in one area is guilty of breaking all of it. Whether in your thoughts or actions, in words or deeds, failing to do something or doing something differently than required is sin, and the consequence of sin is death.

The first four of the Ten Commandments, often called the First Table, tell us our duty to God. It was right that these come first because people have a Creator they should love before they have a neighbor to love. You cannot expect someone to be loyal to their neighbor if they are disloyal to God. The first commandment concerns who is worthy of worship: Jehovah alone. Worshipping anything else is forbidden. Anything less than perfect love, gratitude, reverence, or worship breaks this commandment. Whatever you do, give all the glory to God. The second commandment refers to how we should worship the Lord our God. It is forbidden to make any image or picture of God in any form or for any purpose; or to worship any creature, image, or picture. But the spiritual meaning of this command goes much further. All kinds of superstition are also forbidden, as well as using human inventions in worshipping God. The third commandment concerns the manner of worship, that it be with all possible reverence and seriousness. False oaths are forbidden. Any disrespectful language toward God is a serious violation of this command. It doesn't matter if you misuse God’s word or treat sacred things disrespectfully; these actions break this commandment, and there is no benefit, honor, or pleasure in them. God will not hold guiltless anyone who uses his name carelessly. The form of the fourth commandment, “Remember,” shows that it was not being given for the first time, but that people already knew about it. One day in seven should be kept holy. Six days are for work and business, but not so as to neglect serving God and caring for your soul. On those days you must do all your work and leave none to be done on the Sabbath day. Christ allowed works of necessity, charity, and piety; because the sabbath was made for people, and not people for the sabbath; but all works of luxury, vanity, or self-indulgence in any form are forbidden. Trading, paying wages, settling accounts, writing business letters, studying worldly subjects, making unnecessary visits, traveling, or having casual conversations do not keep this day holy to the Lord. Sloth and laziness may be a physical rest, but not a holy one. The Sabbath of the Lord should be a day of rest from work and a time for serving God. The benefits of keeping this holy day, even just for your health and happiness, along with the time it provides for caring for your soul, show how excellent this commandment is. The day is blessed; people are blessed by it and in it. The blessing and direction to keep holy are not limited to the seventh day, but apply to the Sabbath day.

The laws of the Second Table, that is, the last six of the Ten Commandments, explain our duty to ourselves and to one another, and clarify the great commandment: You shall love your neighbor as yourself. Godliness and honesty must go together. The fifth commandment concerns the duties we owe to our family members. Honor your father and mother includes showing respect for them in your actions; obeying their lawful commands; coming when they call you, going where they send you, doing what they ask you, refraining from what they forbid; and this, as children, cheerfully, and from a principle of love. Also submitting to their advice and corrections. Strive to comfort parents and make their old age easy; support them if they need it, which our Savior says is particularly intended in this commandment. Careful observers have noticed a special blessing on obedient children and the opposite on disobedient children. The sixth commandment requires that we regard the lives and safety of others as we do our own. Magistrates and their officers, and witnesses telling the truth, do not break this command. Self-defense is lawful; but many things that are not considered murder by human laws are so before God. Furious passions stirred up by anger or drunkenness are no excuse: murder in duels is even more guilty, which is a horrible result of a proud, vengeful spirit. All fighting, whether for pay, for fame, or out of anger and malice, breaks this command, and the bloodshed involved is murder. Tempting people to vice and crimes that shorten lives may also be included. Misconduct that can break the heart or shorten the lives of parents, spouses, or other relatives is a violation of this command. This commandment forbids all envy, malice, hatred, or anger, all provoking or insulting language. Taking your own life is forbidden here. This commandment requires a spirit of kindness, patience, and forgiveness. The seventh commandment concerns chastity. You should be as afraid of what defiles the body as you are of what destroys it. Anything that tends to pollute your imagination or stir up your passions falls under this law, such as impure pictures, books, conversations, or anything similar. The eighth commandment is the law of love as it relates to the property of others. The portion of worldly things allotted to us, as far as we have obtained them honestly, is the bread that God has given us; for that we should be thankful, content with it, and, by using lawful means, trust Providence for the future. Taking advantage of someone’s ignorance, kindness, or need, and many other things, break God's law, even though they may not be blamed in society. Thieves who plunder kingdoms, even if beyond human justice, will be included in this judgment. Defrauding the public, contracting debts without a plan to pay them, or failing to pay just debts, extravagance, living on charity when it is not needed, taking advantage of the poor in their wages; these and similar things break this commandment, which requires industry, frugality, and contentment, and treating others about worldly property as you would want them to treat you. The ninth commandment concerns our own and our neighbor's reputation. This forbids speaking falsely on any matter, lying, being evasive, or devising ways to deceive your neighbor. Speaking unjustly against your neighbor to harm their reputation; bearing false witness against them, or slandering, backbiting, and gossiping in common conversation; exaggerating what is done wrong, and trying to raise your own reputation at the expense of your neighbor's—all of these things are forbidden. How often this command is broken every day among people of all ranks! The tenth commandment strikes at the root: You shall not covet. The others forbid any desire to do something that will harm your neighbor; this forbids any wrong desire to have what will satisfy yourself.

This law, which is so extensive that we cannot measure it, so spiritual that we cannot evade it, and so reasonable that we cannot find fault with it, will be the rule for God’s future judgment, as it is for present conduct. If judged by this rule, we will find our lives have been spent in transgressions. And with this holy law and an awesome judgment before us, who can despise the gospel of Christ? And knowledge of the law shows our need for repentance. In every believer's heart sin is dethroned and crucified, God’s law is written, and the image of God is renewed. The Holy Spirit enables him to hate sin and flee from it, to love and keep this law in sincerity and truth; nor will he cease to repent.

Moses having entered into thick darkness, God spoke all that follows from here to the end of chapter , which is mostly an explanation of the Ten Commandments. The laws in these verses relate to God's worship. The Israelites are assured of God’s gracious acceptance of their devotions. Under the gospel, people are encouraged to pray everywhere, and wherever God’s people meet to worship him, he will be in the midst of them; there he will come to them, and bless them.
